coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of carbon dots@hierarchical mesoporous ZIF-8 for simultaneous ratiometric fluorescence detection and removal of tetracycline antibiotics
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yan
Wang, Ying
Du, Peiyao
Zhang, Libing
Liu, Yu
Lu, Xiaoquan
coredata.dc:description: Excessive residues of tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) are very harmful to the ecosystems and human health, thus it is urgent to establish an effective method for both detection and removal of TCs. Herein, the blue luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized and modified into a hierarchical mesoporous zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (HZIF-8) which prepared using hydrogel as template to form CDs@HZIF-8 complex. The blue fluorescence of CDs@HZIF-8 at 440 nm was quenched by TCs through internal filtering effect (IFE), meanwhile, the interaction between TCs and Zn2+ led to a significant conformational change of TCs, which enhanced the fluorescence at 536 nm. Based on the reverse change of two fluorescence signals (F 536/F 440), a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed for the detection of TCs. It is worth mentioning that, thanks to the hierarchical porous structure of CDs@HZIF-8, sufficient space is provided for TCs enrichment in the pores and full contact with the skeleton, which improves the adsorption capacity of TCs and significantly enhances the sensitivity of TCs detection. The detection limits of tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and doxycycline (DOX) were 6.56 nM, 29.46 nM, and 30.58 nM, respectively. Additionally, the rational design method was successfully applied to detect TCs in milk samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Tetracycline antibiotics
Adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TCs in milk samples
Optical properties of CDs and CDs@HZIF-8
Feasibility and principle of ratio fluorescence detection for TCs based on CDs@HZIF-8
Determination of TCs in milk samples
Study on adsorption properties of TCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the as-synthesized CDs (inset: the distribution of CDs diameter). (B) SEM image of CDs@HZIF-8 (inset: SEM image of a single CD@HZIF-8). (C) and (D) TEM image of CDs@HZIF-8. (E) FTIR spectra of CDs, HZIF-8 and CDs@HZIF-8. (F) Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns of HZIF-8, CDs@HZIF-8, and simulated ZIF-8.

(A) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs (Inset: photographs of CDs under natural sunlight and UV light). (B) Optical stability and (C) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (D) excitation and emission spectra of CDs@HZIF-8 (Inset: photographs of CDs@HZIF-8 under natural sunlight and UV light). (E) Fluorescence stability and (F) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@HZIF-8 at different excitation wavelengths.

(A) UV–vis absorption of TCs, excitation and emission spectra of CDs@HZIF-8. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@HZIF-8, CDs, HZIF-8 without and with 30 μM TC. (C) CIE chromaticity coordinates of CDs@HZIF-8 without (a) and with (b) 30 μM TC. Inset: Photos of CD@HZIF-8 without (No. 1, 3) and with TC (No. 2, 4) under ultraviolet light (No. 1, 2) and natural light (No. 3, 4), respectively.
The effects of (A) different concentrations of CDs@HZIF-8 (inset: the fluorescence intensity at 536 nm), (B) pH and (C) reaction time on the F 536/F 440 value.

(A) Fluorescence responses of CDs@HZIF-8 in the presence of different concentrations of TC. (B) The linear relationships between F 536/F 440 and the concentrations of TC. (C) Photographs under 365 nm UV light and (D) CIE chromophores of CDs@HZIF-8 after the addition of different concentrations of TC.
Fluorescence response of CDs@HZIF-8 to different antibiotics, molecules and cations in the presence (yellow) and absence (pink) of TC.
Schematic diagram of the preparation and application of ratiometric fluorescent probe based on CDs@HZIF-8 for the detection of tetracycline antibiotics.
Detection of TCs in spiked milk samples by the proposed sensor and UV absorption method.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon dots@hierarchical mesoporous ZIF-8 for simultaneous ratiometric fluorescence detection and removal of tetracycline antibiotics

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Molecular Optoelectronic Science, Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive residues of tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) are very harmful to the ecosystems and human health, thus it is urgent to establish an effective method for both detection and removal of TCs. Herein, the blue luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized and modified into a hierarchical mesoporous zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (HZIF-8) which prepared using hydrogel as template to form CDs@HZIF-8 complex. The blue fluorescence of CDs@HZIF-8 at 440 nm was quenched by TCs through internal filtering effect (IFE), meanwhile, the interaction between TCs and Zn2+ led to a significant conformational change of TCs, which enhanced the fluorescence at 536 nm. Based on the reverse change of two fluorescence signals (F 536/F 440), a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed for the detection of TCs. It is worth mentioning that, thanks to the hierarchical porous structure of CDs@HZIF-8, sufficient space is provided for TCs enrichment in the pores and full contact with the skeleton, which improves the adsorption capacity of TCs and significantly enhances the sensitivity of TCs detection. The detection limits of tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and doxycycline (DOX) were 6.56 nM, 29.46 nM, and 30.58 nM, respectively. Additionally, the rational design method was successfully applied to detect TCs in milk samples.

Herein, CDs@HZIF-8 complex was synthesized for simultaneous ratiometric fluorescence detection and removal of tetracycline antibiotics.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) (including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), doxycycline (DOX)) are a class of common broad-spectrum antibiotics, widely used as animal husbandry, aquaculture, and human treatment of infectious diseases [1–3]. However, the metabolic level of TCs in the body is limited and most of the ingested TCs will be excreted from the body and reenter the environment [4]. Excessive residue and a small amount of continuous ingestion of TCs in the environment can lead to liver damage, gastrointestinal disorders, allergic reactions and other adverse reactions [5,6]. To ensure food safety and consumer health, some countries have set maximum residue limits (MRLs) for TCs in food. For example, the US Food and Drug Administration and the European Union established MRLs of TCs in milk at 676 nM and 225 nM, respectively [7]. Therefore, it is a pressing requirement to develop a simple and reliable sensing platform for the detection and removal of TCs from the environment.

So far, numerous methods have been developed to detect TCs, such as capillary electrophoresis [8], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [9], high performance liquid chromatography [10], colorimetry [11] and fluorescence [12–14], etc. Among these approaches, fluorescence is considered as a prior alternative for TCs detection because of its advantages of simplicity, sensitivity, easy operation, and low-cost [15]. However, traditional fluorescence sensors respond to the change of a single fluorescence signal in the analyte and they are susceptible to the interference from the background and environment [16]. Fortunately, ratiometric sensors can eliminate the interference by providing an internal self-calibration system to simultaneously collect fluorescence signals at two different emission wavelengths to improve the accuracy. At present, several nanomaterials have been combined with Eu3+ to prepare ratiometric fluorescence sensors for TCs detection [17–20]. Despite these materials are effective for quantitative detection of TCs, they cannot be used as adsorbents to remove TCs from the water environment. Integrating TCs detection and removal into the material remains a challenge.

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) as an emerging class of porous materials constructed from the self-assembly of organic ligands and metal ions/clusters [21]. Among them, zeolitic imidazolate frameworks-8 (ZIF-8) is one of the most studied MOF materials. Due to its high specific surface area, adjustable pore structure [22] and excellent properties such as easy synthesis, good stability under boiling water, sodium hydroxide and even high pressure, ZIF-8 has garnered much interest in sensing, separation, gas storage and catalysis [23–25]. However, the small pore size of microporous ZIF-8 synthesized by conventional methods limits its application, while the layered porous ZIF-8 can be synthesized by template method [26]. As a type of carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have been attracting substantial attention in the fields of biosensing, drug delivery, and fluorescence sensing recently owing to their superior optical physical properties, high quantum fluorescence yield, and excellent biocompatibility [27–29]. Moreover, it has been reported that functional materials can be obtained by loading fluorescent nanoparticles into MOF through encapsulation [30]. The integration of MOFs with the CDs can not only effectively improve the stability of pure CDs, but also endows the constructed sensors with a high accuracy in the detection process.

In this strategy, hydrogels were used as templates to synthesize hierarchical porous ZIF-8 (HZIF-8) with mesoporous structures. The hydrogel template was formed through hydrogen bonding after the hot melt liquid of salicylic acid and melamine was cooled, and the hydrogel was decomposed at a higher temperature to remove the hydrogel template and produce mesoporous pores in the ZIF framework [31]. In addition, blue luminescent CDs were prepared with citric acid and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials, and finally the CDs was loaded into HZIF-8 through post-synthesis modification to form CDs@HZIF-8 complex due to the interaction between N atom in HZIF-8 and the functional groups of –OH/–COOH in CDs by hydrogen bonding and the favorable pore structure of HZIF-8 promote the binding of CDs and HZIF-8. Using CDs@HZIF-8 as a probe, a ratio-type fluorescence sensor was constructed to simultaneously detect and remove TCs ( Scheme 1). The blue fluorescence of CDs@HZIF-8 at 440 nm was quenched by TCs through internal filtering effect (IFE), and the conformation of TCs was changed significantly due to the interaction between TCs and Zn2+, thus enhancing the yellow-green fluorescence at 536 nm. The coordination between IFE and conformational changes resulted in two reversible fluorescence signal changes, and the fluorescent color gradually changed from blue to yellowish green. In addition, due to the hierarchical mesoporous structure of CDs@HZIF-8, the enrichment of TCs can be induced in the hole of CDs@HZIF-8, which makes TCs fully contact with the material, thus significantly improving the adsorption and sensing efficiency of TCs. Eventually, this strategy has been successfully applied to the determination of TCs in milk samples.

HZIF-8 was synthesized with minor modifications in the same way as before [31]. 0.2610 g of salicylic acid and 0.2381 g of melamine were added to 10 mL of distilled water which contained 0.4463 g of zinc nitrate hexahydrate, and the mixture was stirred for 15 min at 60 °C. The above 10 mL of hot solution was poured into 35 mL of hexane (containing 2.50 g of the Span 85 surfactant) as the oil phase, and emulsified using magnetic stirring at 60 °C for 1 h, then transferred to the ice water bath container, and the mixture was stirred for another 30 min to promote the formation of gel. Finally, 2-methylimidazole aqueous solution (1 mol/L) was added to the emulsion and continued stirring for 12 h. The prepared aqueous gel MOF was collected by centrifugation and suspended in 75 °C water for 6 h, then the hydrogel template was removed. HZIF-8 was centrifuged and washed repeatedly with ethanol, dried at 80 °C for 12 h for subsequent use.

The CDs was encapsulated in hierarchical mesoporesic HZIF-8 by post-synthesis modification. Briefly, 50 mg of HZIF-8 was dispersed in 5 mL of ethanol, and then 1 mL of the synthesized CDs was added. The mixture was continuously stirred for 6 h, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and fully washed with ethanol to remove the unencapsulated CDs and dried overnight at 70 °C. Finally, the obtained CDs@HZIF-8 was redispersed into distilled water to form a 10 mg/mL suspension for subsequent use.

100 μL of CDs@HZIF-8 suspension (10 mg/mL) was added to a 4 mL quartz plate with different concentrations of TCs (0.5–60 μM) and then the mixed solution was diluted to 2 mL with Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 8, 10 mM). Under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, the fluorescence intensity was recorded after 1 min of reaction at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: The milk samples were purchased from a supermarket in Tianjin. According to the reported method, the milk samples were pretreated with minor modifications [7,32]. Firstly, 5 mL of milk samples were added to 10 mL glass vials, followed by adding 1% (v/v) trichloroacetic acid (50 μL) and chloroform (50 μL), and then the samples were rotated for 5 min. Then, the samples were ultrasonic for 20 min and centrifugated at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to remove organic substances such as proteins and lipids. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to obtain filtrate. The filtrate was collected and diluted 50 times with Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 8, 10 mM). Finally, a specific concentration of TCs was added to the pre-treated milk samples, and the established method and ultraviolet absorption analysis were used for detection TCs to evaluate the applicability by standard addition method.

To evaluate the enrichment effect of CDs@HZIF-8, the adsorption isothermal performance of CDs@HZIF-8 for TCs in aqueous solution was studied. In a brown glass bottle, 1 mg/mL of CDs@HZIF-8 adsorbent was mixed with known specific concentration of TCs for 4 h, and the concentration of residual TCs in the supernatant was determined by UV–visible spectrophotometer. The equilibrium adsorption capacity Qe (mg/g) of TCs was calculated by the following formula 1:
(1) Qe = (C0-Ce) V/M
Where C0 and Ce (mg/L) represent the concentrations of TCs at initial and equilibrium time. V (L) is the volume of solution. M (g) is the mass of CDs@HZIF-8.

Using hydrogel as template, graded mesoporous HZIF-8 without fluorescence was synthesized by hot solution-cooling method, and the synthesized blue fluorescence CD was loaded into HZIF-8 to prepare the blue fluorescence CDs@HZIF-8 complex. CDs have a good dispersion and a narrow particle size distribution with an average diameter of 2.58 nm ( Fig. 1A). Fig. S1 shows the formation of cubic HZIF-8 with a size of about 200 nm. The morphology of HZIF-8 is different from the rhombic dodecahedron ZIF-8 prepared by traditional solvothermal method, which is attributed to the rich amine function of melamine as a component of hydrogel template. When the concentration of zinc ions is different, the existence of excessive amine group can lead to the difference of HZIF-8 morphology [31,33]. The morphology of CDs@HZIF-8 is still cubic, indicating that the loading of CD did not damage the morphology of HZIF-8 (Fig. 1B). Furthermore, the TEM image clearly identifies the mesoporous structure of CDs@HZIF-8 (Fig. 1C) and the loaded CDs (Fig. 1D). It can be seen that CDs@HZIF-8 is still a porous material with both micropores and mesopores after loading CDs from the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. S2A). After loading CDs, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of the material decreased from 1087 m2/g to 1044 m2/g, which also verified the successful loading of CDs. In addition, the average pore size of CDs@HZIF-8 is about 4.7 nm (Fig. S2B), and the large mesoporous structure provides conditions for enrichment of analytes. In the FTIR spectrum of CDs, the wide absorption at about 3400 cm-1 brings tensile vibration from N − H and O−H, the absorption peak at about 1700 cm-1 is attributed to stretching vibration of CO. The small absorption peaks at 1350 cm-1 and 1220 cm-1 correspond to the bending vibration of C−N and stretching vibration of C−O [34], respectively. These results indicate the existence of hydrophilic groups such as −OH, −COOH and −NH2 on the surface of CDs (Fig. 1E). No characteristic peaks of salicylic acid and melamine were found at about 3500 cm-1 in the FTIR spectrum of HZIF-8, suggesting the removal of the hydrogel template (Fig. S3). The characteristic peaks of HZIF-8 and CDs@HZIF-8 appeared at 450 cm-1 due to Zn−N stretching, indicating the successful formation of HZIF-8 skeleton. Fig. 1F showed X-ray powder diffraction patterns of HZIF-8 with and without CDs loading. All peaks of the two spectra overlap and match well with that of the simulated one derived from the ZIF-8 single crystal structure, indicating that CDs loading does not destroy the high crystallinity of HZIF-8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and CDs@HZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous solution of synthesized CDs has an UV absorption peak at 370 nm ( Fig. 2A), which can be attributed to the n-π * transitions of CN and CO. Strong emission was observed at 440 nm under excitation at 370 nm, and the excitation wavelength overlapped with the absorption wavelength. When exposed to ultraviolet light, the prepared CDs appears intense blue light (illustration of Fig. 2A). As shown in Fig. 2B, CDs can still maintain high fluorescence intensity under 120 min continuous irradiation, indicating that CDs have good stability. The fluorescence spectras of CDs at different excitation wavelengths of 320 nm–400 nm were recorded (Fig. 2C), the results showed that the blue luminescent CDs is non-excited, which is due to the uniform surface defects and dispersion of CDs [30,35]. As for CDs@HZIF-8, under the excitation of 370 nm, strong blue emission was also observed at 440 nm (Fig. 2D). Under the irradiation of ultraviolet light, the prepared CDs@HZIF-8 also emitted strong blue light, which further demonstrated the successful loading of CDs. In addition, CDs@HZIF-8 also has excellent fluorescence stability and non-excitation (Fig. 2E, F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and principle of ratio fluorescence detection for TCs based on CDs@HZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: For the following reasons, CDs@HZIF-8 was chosen to develop a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for TCs detection. First, the UV absorption spectrum of TCs overlaps with the excitation band of CDs@HZIF-8 ( Fig. 3A), indicating that TCs may quench the fluorescence of CDs@HZIF-8 at 440 nm through the internal filtering effect (IFE), since IFE depends on the quenching agent absorption and the spectral overlap of the excitation or emission of fluorophores [36,37]. Secondly, taking TC as an example, the detection mechanism of TC is further discussed. According to Fig. 3B, there was no fluorescence emission peak at 440 nm for HZIF-8 alone, however, an obvious emission peak appeared after CDs loading, which further proved the successful loading of CDs. For CDs alone, it was found that the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was quenched after adding TC, meanwhile, the fluorescence at 536 nm was not significantly changed. The addition of TC to pure HZIF-8 resulted in a new fluorescence peak at 536 nm, while the peak at 440 nm did not change. When TC was added to CDs@HZIF-8 complex, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was quenched, and the fluorescence peak at 536 nm was enhanced. Because OTC and DOX have similar structures and UV absorption bands to TC, the same fluorescence change tendency occurs when they are added to CDs@HZIF-8 (Fig. S4). The mechanism of fluorescence enhancement at 536 nm was studied by absorption and fluorescence spectra of TC reaction with Zn2+, 2-methylimidazole and CDs@HZIF-8. It can be seen from Fig. S5 that the absorption band of TC is centered around 350 nm, and there is very weak fluorescence around 600 nm. The absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of TC were affected by 2-methylimidazole and Zn2+. The interaction between TC and 2-methylimidazole results in a red shift in TC absorption peak, while the interaction between TC and Zn2+ results in a significant increase in absorbance and fluorescence intensity, which may be due to the combination of Zn2+ and TC to form a complex. When TC reacts with CDs@HZIF-8, the absorbance of the system is slightly redshifted and obviously enhanced, and the fluorescence emission intensity is also obviously enhanced. In addition, the interaction between TC and Zn2+ was studied and reported by predecessors through circular dichroism spectrometer [38,39], the results further confirmed that Zn2+ could bind to the ring of TC, resulting in a significant change in the conformation of TC and the enhancement of fluorescence intensity at 536 nm. According to the fluorescence lifetime experiment, the average fluorescence life of CDs does not change significantly after the addition of TC, thus the fluorescence quenching at 440 nm was considered to be static quenching caused by internal filtering effect. However, the average fluorescence life of CDs@HZIF-8 decreases from 4.88 ns to 3.26 ns after the addition of TC due to the conformation change caused by the combination of Zn2+ and TC (Fig. S6 and Table S1). Therefore, the overall detection process of CDs@HZIF-8 was considered to be a dynamic process. Therefore, CDs and HZIF-8 in CDs@HZIF-8 complex can be used as TCs recognition units to produce the reverse change of the two fluorescence signals, providing conditions for proportional fluorescence sensing (F 536/F 440). Thirdly, as can be seen from the chromaticity diagram of the International del’Eclairage Commission (CIE), there is a great color difference in the fluorescence between CDs@HZIF-8 (0.1721, 0.1154) and CDs@HZIF-8/TC (0.3181, 0.4498) (Fig. 3C). CDs@HZIF-8 and CDs@HZIF-8/TC is colorless and transparent under natural light. The present proportional fluorescence sensor CDs@HZIF-8 can display a significant color evolution from blue to yellowish-green under ultraviolet light when TC exists (inset in Fig. 3C), which has great advantages for the visual analysis of TC.

To acquire the high sensing performance of CDs@HZIF-8 system for TCs detection, the concentration of CDs@HZIF-8, pH and reaction time were optimized. As illustrated in Fig. 4A, with the final concentration of CDs@HZIF-8 increasing from 0.1 mg/mL to 0.75 mg/mL, F 536/F 440 gradually tends to be stable. Moreover, when the concentration exceeds 0.5 mg/mL, F 536/F 440 has little change trend. It also can be found from the Fig. 4A inset, when the final concentration of CDs@HZIF-8 reaches 0.5 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity at 536 nm also tends to be stable. Thus, considering the green environmental protection and material saving, 0.5 mg/mL is selected as the final concentration of CDs@HZIF-8. Then, the Tris-HCl buffer was used to optimize the pH of the sensor system (Fig. 4B). We found that the F 536/F 440 value of CDs@HZIF-8 was not affected by pH when the pH was in the range of 5–10. However, F 536/F 440 showed obvious response toward 3 μM TCs at different pH values, and reached the maximum value when the pH value between 8 and 9. The F 536/ F 440 decreased when the pH continued to increase, it is attributed to the decomposition of TCs under alkaline conditions, thus breaking the structure of TCs [40]. Therefore, pH = 8.0 is regarded as the optimal pH value in this study. Subsequently, the response time of the CDs@HZIF-8 system toward TCs was studied. As can be seen from Fig. 4C, the F 536/F 440 increased rapidly within 1 min and tended to stabilize to a constant value after adding 3 μM TCs to CDs@HZIF-8, indicating that the reaction was basically completed. Therefore, the final reaction time was 1 min.

Under the optimal conditions, the performance of CDs@HZIF-8 as a ratiometric sensing platform for TCs detection was evaluated, taking TC as an example. After introducing different concentrations of TC (0.5–60 μM) into the CDs@HZIF-8 sensing system, the fluorescence emission of CDs@HZIF-8 at 440 nm gradually quenched while that at 536 nm gradually increased ( Fig. 5A). With the increase of TC concentration, TC gradually occupies the space of CDs@HZIF-8. In the range of 0.5–50μM, the concentrations of TC and F 536/F 440 are linearly correlated in two different linear ranges due to the spatial limitation of CDs@HZIF-8 (Fig. 5B). Formula (2) is used when the TC concentration range is 0.5–7 μM, and it follows formula (3) when the TC concentration range is 7–50 μM.
(2)
F 536/F 440 = 0.3691[TC] + 0.2303 R 2=0·9889
(3)
F 536/F 440 = 0.07624[TC] + 2.2184 R 2=0·9936

Based on 3σ/slope, the limit of detection (LOD) for TC was calculated as low as 6.56 nM, which is significantly lower than the allowable concentration (225 nM) stipulated by the European Union [17]. More importantly, under UV light irradiation, a significant color change from blue to yellowish-green was instantly and gradually appeared with the increase of TC concentration (Fig. 5C). The corresponding color change was also demonstrated using the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) chromaticity (Fig. 5D). As TC concentration increased from 0.5 μM to 60 μM, CIE coordinates moved continuously from blue (0.2074, 0.1891) to yellow-green (0.3380, 0.4726). The obvious color change provides a prerequisite for the development of portable TC vision sensors. In addition, we also studied the detection performances of CDs@HZIF-8 against OTC and DOX, found that the introduction of different concentrations of OTC (0.5–60 μM) or DOX (0.5–55 μM) into CDs@HZIF-8 system also reduced the fluorescence peak at 440 nm and enhanced the fluorescence peak at 536 nm (Fig. S7A, C). The concentration of OTC was linear with F536/F440 in the range of 0.5–7 μM (R2 = 0.9903) and 7–40 μM (R2 = 0.9845) (Fig. S7B), and the LOD was calculated as 29.46 nM. Fig. S7D showed that the concentration of DOX was linear to F536/F440 in the concentration range of 0.5–10 μM (R2 = 0.9832) and 10–40 μM (R 2 = 0.9883) with LOD of 30.58 nM. Compared with some previously reported systems for detecting TCs, the proportional fluorescence sensor we constructed had a lower LOD (Table S2).

In order to evaluate the specificity of CDs@HZIF-8 toward TCs under optimal conditions, the effects of other antibiotics (CIP, SMZ, PFX, EFX) and several potential interfering substances (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, VB1, Glu, Lactose, GSH, Try, Lys, Tyr, His, and BSA) were investigated. As presented in Fig. 6, there was no significant change in the fluorescence response (F 536/F 440) of these potentially disturbing species, while F 536/F 440 showed an obvious fluorescence response after the addition of TCs. In addition, the anti-interference experiments were carried out by mixing interference substances (concentration consistent with the selection experiment, 300 μM) with TC (30 μM). It can be seen that the sensor performance is still not disturbed by other substances. Above results indicate that CDs@HZIF-8 has good selectivity and anti-interference for TCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TCs in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicability of the present ratiometric system for the detection of TCs, the studies were extended to milk sample. First, the milk samples were pretreated according to the method described in the experimental procedure, and then the milk samples were added with different concentrations (5 and 20 μM) of TCs by the standard addition method. As shown in Table 1, the spiked recovery of the ratio fluorescence sensor we proposed was in the range of 94.83–101.79% and the relative standard deviation was lower than 6.61%. And, the results obtained from the fluorescence sensor we proposed were well consistent with those achieved by conventional UV absorption method, indicating that CDs@HZIF-8 possesses a promising operability of TCs quantification in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on adsorption properties of TCs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to detection, the removal of residual TCs in the water system is also of utmost important, thus the adsorption performance of CDs@HZIF-8 on TCs is further studied. After TCs was adsorbed by CDs@HZIF-8, the amount of residual TCs in the supernatant was calculated by the absorbance at 352 nm. According to adsorption isotherm experiment, the adsorption capacity of CDs@HZIF-8 to TC, OTC and DOX can reach 1974.03 mg/g, 1328.42 mg/g, and 1441.44 mg/g, respectively. The adsorption performance of CDs@HZIF-8 on TCs was compared with that of other porous materials (Table S3), and the results indicated that the adsorption performance of CDs@HZIF-8 toward TCs was quite high. Such a high adsorption capacity of CDs@HZIF-8 for TCs may be attributed to the layered mesoporous structure of CDs@HZIF-8, which provides sufficient space for the adsorption of TCs and enables the enrichment of TCs in the pores of CDs@HZIF-8, thus improving the detection sensitivity and removal efficiency of TCs in the water environment.

In summary, the hierarchical mesoporous HZIF-8 was synthesized using hydrogel as template and combined with blue fluorescent CDs to form CDs@HZIF-8 complex. The synergy of the IFE of TCs and the interaction between TCs and Zn2+ resulted in two kinds of reverse fluorescence changes, which provided the prerequisite for future visual perception. In addition, the hierarchical mesoporous structure and large specific surface area of CDs@HZIF-8 provide sufficient space for enrichment and adsorption of analytes, which can effectively remove residual TCs in the water environment. The sensing system has also been applied to the determination of TCs in milk samples with satisfactory recoveries, making it promising in food safety monitoring. Our work not only highlights a simple and effect strategy for fluorescence detection and visual perception toward TCs, but also can effectively remove residual TCs in water environment during the detection process.

Yan Li: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ying Wang: Formal analysis, Methodology. Peiyao Du: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Libing Zhang: Formal analysis. Yu Liu: Formal analysis. Xiaoquan Lu: Formal analysis, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22001193, 22127803 and 22174110), the Industrial Support Plan of Gansu Provincial Department of Education (2021cyzc-01), and Special Fund Project for Guiding Local Scientific and Technological Development by the Central Government (No. 2020-2060503-17).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131526.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yan Li is a graduate student at School of Science, Tianjin University, China. Her major research interest is the development of fluorescence sensors.
Ying Wang is a graduate student at School of Science, Tianjin University, China. Her main research interests are the synthesis and application of electrocatalysts.
Peiyao Du is a professor at Northwest A&F University, China. She received her Ph.D. from Nankai University in 2018. Her current research interests include optical sensors and material science.
Libing Zhang is a professor at Tianjin University, China. His current research interests include optical sensors.
Yu Liu is a professor at Tianjin University, China. Her current research interests include optical sensors.

Xiaoquan Lu is the director of Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu, China. He has been the Cheung Kong Professor of Ministry of Education (China) since 2013. His research interest focuses on electroanalytical chemistry, biosensors, etc.
